message:
fix(sync): preserve unflushed tombstones across startup auto-recovery, and hoist tombstone helpers into sync::mod

Two coupled changes, kept atomic because the startup-path preservation
fix calls directly into the helpers that this commit moves:

1) Hoist tombstone-preservation helpers from `cli/commands/sync.rs` into
   `sync/mod.rs`. `PreservedTombstone`, `snapshot_tombstones`,
   `restore_tombstones`, `tombstones_missing_from_jsonl_tombstones`, and
   `get_tombstone_ids_from_jsonl` now live alongside the rest of the
   sync primitives as `pub(crate)`. The CLI module re-imports them
   unchanged, so the user-facing `br sync --rebuild` and `br sync
   --force` paths keep their existing behavior. This is a pure move
   with one adjustment: logging switched from `warn!/debug!` macros
   (which relied on the CLI's tracing import) to `tracing::warn!` /
   `tracing::debug!` paths so the library crate does not inherit a
   tracing macro from the binary.

2) Use those helpers to preserve unflushed tombstones across startup
   auto-recovery. Previously `open_sqlite_storage_with_recovery_strategy`
   handled two anomaly cases — `detect_recoverable_open_anomaly`
   returning `Some(anomaly)`, and the probe itself erroring out — by
   immediately `drop(storage)` and calling `rebuild_database_from_jsonl`.
   The rebuild only restores what is in the JSONL, so any tombstone
   that the user had deleted but not yet flushed (`br delete <id>
   --no-auto-flush` or an aborted flush) was silently wiped: the JSONL
   still showed the issue open, the rebuild imported it as open, and
   the deletion-retention state was lost. The explicit `br sync
   --rebuild` path was already fixed in 97a085c / 31f9b72; the startup
   auto-recovery path had the same bug but a different entry point.

   The new `preserved_unflushed_tombstones` snapshots tombstones from
   the anomalous-but-queryable storage before dropping it (the anomaly
   detector flags duplicate config/metadata/schema rows — none of
   which break a plain `SELECT … FROM issues`), filters out the ones
   already present in the JSONL as tombstones (the rebuild will bring
   those back on its own), and returns the rest. After the rebuild,
   `restore_tombstones` re-inserts the preserved rows atomically
   inside a write transaction and marks them dirty so the next flush
   re-exports them. Both the "anomaly-flagged" and "probe-erroring"
   arms now take this path.

   Best-effort semantics: any failure inside `preserved_unflushed_tombstones`
   (snapshot failed, JSONL tombstone ID read failed) degrades to an
   empty preservation set plus a debug/warn log, so the rebuild never
   fails because of the preservation step. Worst case is the old
   behavior (no preservation); best case we rescue the unflushed
   tombstones the old code dropped.

   Also reorder the JSONL-hash capture inside
   `open_storage_with_startup_config`: we now hash the JSONL BEFORE
   the import instead of after. If a non-`br` writer (e.g. `git pull`,
   which does not respect `.write.lock`) rewrites the JSONL between
   our import and our flush, the post-import hash would equal the
   already-modified file while the storage only holds the
   pre-modification content — the staleness check at flush time
   would silently wave the flush through and overwrite the modified
   file with stale data. Hashing first makes any such mid-operation
   rewrite surface as a `SyncConflict` at flush time instead of data
   loss.

3) E2E regression `e2e_startup_auto_recovery_preserves_unflushed_tombstones`:
   creates two issues, flushes so JSONL shows both open, deletes one
   with `--no-auto-flush` (tombstone only in DB), injects duplicate
   `config.issue_prefix` rows directly into the SQLite DB so the next
   open trips `detect_recoverable_open_anomaly`, then runs `br show
   <deleted-id> --json` and asserts the record comes back with
   `"status": "tombstone"`. Without this fix, the rebuild imports the
   JSONL's open row and the assertion fails with `status: "open"`.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
